1: mizbeach miqtar qetoret (מִזְבַּח מִקְטַר קְטֹרֶת, H4196+H7004) — an altar specifically for burning incense. Mizbeach (altar) derives from zabach (to slaughter), yet this altar receives no blood: it is the altar of approach by fragrance, not sacrifice. The acacia-and-gold construction places it structurally in the outer holy place but functionally at the threshold of the Most Holy, standing before the veil. Hebrew distinguishes it consistently from mizbeach ha'olah (the burnt-offering altar in the court), so every occurrence of this noun carries the distinct register of priestly nearness rather than atonement.
2: amah orko ve'amah rochbo (a cubit long and a cubit wide) — the incense altar is the smallest altar: one cubit square, two cubits high, shaped like a tall column. The proportion — taller than wide — suits an altar whose purpose is vertical ascent of smoke rather than horizontal surface for offering. Qarnot (its horns) are integral, not added separately; they are mimmennu yihyeh (of one piece with it), echoing the same design language for the mercy seat (Exodus 25:19).
3: zehav tahor (זָהָב טָהוֹר, pure/refined gold) — the incense altar is overlaid with the same pure gold as the ark and the mercy seat, not merely the table of showbread's ordinary gold. Tahor (H2889) carries connotations of ritual and moral purity as well as physical refinement; its application to the metal signals that this object belongs to the innermost sanctity-register. The zer zahav (golden crown/molding) running around its edge matches the ark and table (Exodus 25:11,24), creating a visual unity among the holy vessels.
4: shetei tabba'ot zahav (two gold rings) — two rings on either side for carrying poles, the same portable design as the ark and table. The incense altar must be movable without being touched, maintaining holiness protocol. Its location al shetei tzala'otav (on its two sides) — the rings placed at the frame (lechatztzelo) — is a technical detail ensuring stable transport without profaning contact.
5: atzei shittim (acacia wood) — the same material used for every portable sacred object in the tabernacle. Acacia (shittah, H7848) is a dense, termite-resistant hardwood native to Sinai; its use throughout likely reflects both availability and the tradition that incorruptibility belongs to what is set apart for God. Gold overlay transforms this desert wood into the appearance of pure metal.
6: lifnei happarochet (before/in front of the veil) — the incense altar stands outside the veil but immediately before it, facing the ark and mercy seat within. The phrase lifnei hakaporet (before the mercy seat) makes its orientation explicit: Aaron burns incense before the presence, not merely before furniture. The spatial language establishes that priestly prayer, represented by the incense column, is directionally aimed at the locus of divine presence.
7: qetoret samim (קְטֹרֶת סַמִּים, fragrant/spiced incense) — Aaron burns this compound incense every morning when he tends the lamps (beheitivo et hanerot). Qetoret (H7004) derives from qatar (to go up in smoke, to make an offering by fire); the fragrance rising becomes the olfactory counterpart to visible fire. Samim (H5561) denotes aromatic spices in general; here specifying that only the prescribed compound (v34-38) is acceptable. The simultaneity of incense and lamp-tending frames the priest's morning service as a multi-sensory act of holy attention.
8: beha'alot Aharon et hanerot bein ha'arbayim (when Aaron sets up the lamps at twilight) — the incense is also burned at evening, making it qetoret tamid (perpetual/continual incense) before the LORD throughout generations. Bein ha'arbayim (between the evenings) is the standard phrase for the late-afternoon hour, used also for the Passover sacrifice timing (Exodus 12:6). The word tamid (H8548, continual/perpetual) appears as the signature of a never-ceasing obligation — also describing the daily burnt offering, the showbread, and the lamp.
9: qetoret zarah (קְטֹרֶת זָרָה, strange/unauthorized/foreign incense) — zarah (H2114, strange, unauthorized, belonging to another) is the precise legal term for any offering not conforming to divine specification. This prohibition is violated in Leviticus 10:1 by Nadab and Abihu who offer esh zarah (strange fire), with fatal consequence. The altar's singularity of function enforces singularity of worship — the incense altar must do only one thing.
10: vekipper Aharon al-qarnot (Aaron shall make atonement on its horns) — once a year, on the Day of Atonement, the incense altar receives blood from the chattat (sin offering) applied to its horns. Kaphar (H3722) — the foundational atonement verb of Levitical theology — means to cover, to ransom, to make good a debt. The phrase qodesh qodashim hu laYHWH (it is most holy to the LORD) is the highest holiness designation in the tabernacle system, shared only with the ark-chamber and the sin-offering flesh.
11: A new speech-formula begins: vaydabber YHWH el-Mosheh lemor (the LORD spoke to Moses saying). The census section (vv11-16) is an independent divine speech inserted into the tabernacle instructions, connecting the counting of Israel to a redemption requirement. Its placement here — between altar design and laver instructions — keeps the practical and the theological inseparable throughout the tabernacle legislation.
12: kofer nafsho laYHWH (כֹּפֶר נַפְשׁוֹ לַיהוָה, a ransom for his life/soul to the LORD) — when counting Israel, each man over 20 must pay a ransom, or a plague will follow. Kofer (H3724) is a payment that substitutes for a life; it is cognate to kaphar (atonement). Nefesh (H5315, life/soul/person) here designates the individual as mortal and accountable before God. The census itself is dangerous because it makes visible the whole of God's possession; the ransom acknowledges that Israel's life belongs to God and can only be counted by those who have paid for the right to be numbered under his protection. The same theology explains David's catastrophic census in 2 Sam 24.
13: machatsit hashekel beshekel hakodesh (מַחֲצִית הַשֶּׁקֶל בְּשֶׁקֶל הַקֹּדֶשׁ, a half-shekel by the shekel of the sanctuary) — the unit is precisely specified: esrim gerah hashekel (20 gerahs per shekel). Gerah (H1626) is the smallest standard weight unit; the sanctuary shekel is the official cultic standard, slightly heavier than the commercial shekel, ensuring no shortfall in the holy offering. The half-shekel becomes a fixed annual temple tax in Second Temple Judaism (cf. Matthew 17:24-27 where Jesus addresses the temple-tax collectors — a direct continuation of this legislation).
14: kol ha'oveir al hapequdim (everyone who passes over among those counted) — the census applies to males from twenty years and above, the military service age (cf. Numbers 1:3). The phrase al hapequdim (among those counted) is the root of the book title Bemidbar (Numbers), which opens with a similar census using the same vocabulary. Paqad (H6485) means to appoint, to visit for inspection, to muster — each man becomes accountable before the divine commander.
15: lo yarbeh vehadal lo yamit (the rich shall not give more and the poor shall not give less) — the census ransom is radically egalitarian. Wealth offers no advantage; poverty grants no exemption. Each soul has the same price before God. This flattening of economic hierarchy at the most sacred transaction — the ransom of one's life — is unparalleled in ANE temple-tax systems, where contributions were typically tiered by wealth or status.
16: kesef hakkippurim (the atonement money) — the ransom silver is collected and used for the service of the tent of meeting. It becomes a zikkaron (memorial/reminder) before the LORD. Zikkaron (H2146) — from zachar (to remember) — marks objects or rituals that prompt divine attention: the blowing of trumpets is a zikkaron, the stones on the high priest's shoulders are a zikkaron. Here the accumulated ransom becomes an ongoing reminder of Israel's redemption and God's claim on every life.
17: A third divine speech begins: the bronze laver (kior nechoshet). Kior (H3595) is a basin or pot; nechoshet (H5178, bronze/copper) is the metal of the outer court, distinct from the gold of the inner sanctuary. The laver stands between the tent of meeting and the altar, requiring priests to pass it on the way from slaughter to service.
18: kior nechoshet vekhan nechoshto (a bronze basin with a bronze stand) — the laver has a kan (stand/base, H3653) specified as also bronze, distinguishing it from the later Solomonic lavers on wheeled stands (1 Kings 7:27-37). Its placement bein ohel moed uvein hamizbeach (between the tent of meeting and the altar) is spatial theology: no priest moves from sacrificing animal to serving before God without washing. The laver is not optional; it is the required transition between blood work and holy service.
19: rachazu yadeihem veragleihem (they shall wash their hands and their feet) — the specific body parts indicate the limbs of action (hands) and movement (feet), the parts that have touched the work of slaughter and walking through the court. The washing is not ablution for defilement; it is preparation for approach. The penalty for unwashed approach is velo yamutu (so that they do not die) — the laver is a death-prevention measure, not a cleansing rite in the Lev 11-15 impurity sense.
20: uvekarvotam el-hamizbeach lesharet (when they come near the altar to minister) — the priestly washing applies at two moments: entering the tent of meeting, and approaching the altar for service. Sharet (H8334, to minister/serve) is the term for priestly and Levitical service throughout the Torah; it marks the distinctly cultic form of work — consecrated duty before the LORD, not slave labor.
21: chok olam lo ulezer'o ledorotam (a perpetual statute for him and his descendants throughout their generations) — the laver ordinance is chok (H2706, statute/binding rule), the same legal category as the Passover and the Day of Atonement. Olam (H5769, forever/perpetuity) here means the permanent character of priestly obligation — it does not expire with any generation of priests.
22: A fourth speech opens the anointing oil instructions. The recipe is presented as divine prescription with exact quantities — a unique feature among the tabernacle commands. The oil is not improvised or approximately mixed; it is a fixed formula whose precision mirrors the exactness of the tabernacle construction itself.
23: mor deror (מֹר דְּרוֹר, free-flowing myrrh) — deror (H1865) means flowing freely, liberty; this is liquid (rather than solid) myrrh, the premier fragrance of the ancient Near East. Qinnemon besem (sweet cinnamon) — an imported spice from South/Southeast Asian trade routes. Qaneh bosem (aromatic cane/calamus) — qaneh (H7070) is the reed-stalk; with bosem (fragrance) it designates a specific aromatic reed. The exact botanical identification of each ingredient has been debated in rabbinic and scholarly literature; what is fixed is their exotic, imported character — the tabernacle draws on the fragrant wealth of the whole earth.
24: qiddah (cassia, H6916) — a bark spice, a cheaper relative of cinnamon, present in the Egyptian and Mesopotamian luxury spice trade. The hin of olive oil (~4 liters) is the base carrier for all four dry spices. Unlike the incense recipe (which specifies equal parts), the oil recipe gives precise weight ratios for each spice in shekalim (shekels by weight). The formula is irreducible; adding or subtracting any component makes it no longer the holy anointing oil.
25: shemen mishchat qodesh (שֶׁמֶן מִשְׁחַת קֹדֶשׁ, holy anointing oil) — mishchah (H4888) is the noun form of mashach (H4886, to smear/anoint). The verb mashach is the semantic root of mashiach (Messiah, Anointed One). Every application of this oil — to the tabernacle, its furniture, Aaron, and his sons — is an act of meshichah (anointing for office), marking the boundary between common and consecrated. Roqach mirkachat (a perfumer's compound) — roqach (H7543) is the perfumer/apothecary; the phrase signals professional, skilled preparation.
26: umashachta vo et ohel moed (you shall anoint the tent of meeting with it) — the oil consecrates the tent itself, then each article of furniture: ark, table, lampstand, altars. Anointing objects — not just persons — is distinctive in Israel's practice. The theology is that place and tool can be set apart for sacred use; the tent becomes holy ground by divine commission expressed through anointing.
27: The furnishings listed: the table and all its utensils (kelav), the lampstand and its utensils, the incense altar. Each is anointed individually. The Hebrew list is grammatically co-ordinated with ve (and) — a long asyndeton of holiness, each object drawn into the same sacred domain by the same act.
28: The altar of burnt offering with all its utensils, and the laver with its stand — consecrated by the same oil. The anointing extends from the most holy inner furnishings to the outermost court objects; the entire complex becomes a single consecrated space from center to periphery.
29: qiddashta otam vehau qodesh qodashim (you shall consecrate them and they shall be most holy) — qiddash (H6942, to consecrate/make holy) effects a status change: after anointing, the ordinary becomes most holy. The corollary follows: kol hanogea bahem yiqdash (whatever touches them becomes holy). This is contagious holiness — the highest concentration of holiness transmits to contact. The same principle governs the flesh of sin offerings (Leviticus 6:27).
30: ve'et Aharon ve'et banav timshach (and Aaron and his sons you shall anoint) — the same oil that consecrates furniture consecrates persons. The priest is continuous with the tabernacle's holiness system. Lekahano li (to serve as priest to me) — the purpose of priestly anointing is always functional, not honorific: it installs for service, not status.
31: shemen mishchat qodesh yihyeh zeh li ledoroteikhem (this shall be my holy anointing oil throughout your generations) — the formula is fixed for all time. The possessive li (to me/mine) marks the oil as belonging to God; it is given to Israel for use in God's service, not owned by Israel as a religious technology.
32: al basar adam lo yisak (on the body of a common/ordinary person it shall not be poured) — the oil has no therapeutic, cosmetic, or personal use; applying it outside its consecrated purpose is a category violation. Uvemarkavato lo ta'asu kamohu (you shall not make any like it in its proportions) — not even copying the recipe for secular use is permitted. The formula belongs exclusively to the divine domain.
33: asher yirqach kamohu (whoever compounds any like it) — the prohibition has two directions: using the holy oil for ordinary purposes, and making the formula for non-sacral use. Both carry the karet penalty: venichrat me'ammav (he shall be cut off from his people). Karet (H3772, cutting off) is the most serious penalty in Levitical law — exclusion from the covenant community — confirming that the anointing oil is not ceremonial but covenantally constitutive.
34: qach lecha samim (take yourself spices) — the incense recipe is introduced with the same direct-address command as the oil recipe. Four ingredients: nataf (stacte, a myrrh resin drop), shekhelet (onycha, possibly the burnt shell of a sea mollusk), chelbenah (galbanum, a pungent resin), levonah (frankincense, H3828). Levonah appears alone elsewhere as a royal and priestly fragrance (Song of Solomon 3:6; Isaiah 60:6); here it is the fourth in a compound. All four have uncertain exact botanical/zoological identification; what is clear is that they are gathered from across the ancient world's fragrant trade.
35: veAsita ota qetoret roqach (you shall make it a blended incense, the work of a perfumer) — the incense, like the oil, must be professionally compounded, not improvised. Mamulach tahor qodesh (salted/seasoned, pure, holy) — memullach (H4414, seasoned with salt) likely indicates a preservative or binding process; salt in the OT also functions as a covenant symbol (Leviticus 2:13).
36: veshakata mimmenah hadek (you shall grind some of it fine) — the incense is ground to fine powder (dak, H1851) before being placed before the testimony in the tent. The act of grinding makes it immediately combustible for the altar fire. Lifnei ha'edut (before the testimony/covenant) — the ground incense is placed in the tent at the site of the ark and tablets, the very center of covenant presence.
37: vehaqqetoret asher ta'aseh (the incense which you shall make) — explicit prohibition: you shall not make it for yourselves in its proportions. The same exclusivity logic as the anointing oil: the recipe belongs to God. Qodesh tihyeh lecha laYHWH (it shall be most holy to you for the LORD) — both a privilege and a prohibition.
38: ish asher ya'aseh kamohah (any person who makes any like it) — to enjoy the scent personally (lehariyach bo, to smell it) violates the sanctity of the divine fragrance. The karet penalty again: venichrat me'ammav. The incense formula serves as a microcosm of the entire tabernacle's logic: sacred objects are not instruments for human enjoyment but media for divine encounter, operating in the opposite direction — from creation toward Creator, not from Creator toward human pleasure.
